### Post by Mastermind1980 on 2009-07-01
First, I wanted to try linux. But no internet for my wpn111, after 5 days trying to get internet I decided to use Kubuntu to run in VirtualBox. But now I can't set the resolution higher then 800x600..... I need to edit some xorg.conf file but every time I change it and reboot, It starts running in low graphs mode. Pls help me, heres my xorg:

# xorg.conf (X.Org X Window System server configuration file)
#
# This file was generated by dexconf, the Debian X Configuration tool, using
# values from the debconf database.
#
# Edit this file with caution, and see the xorg.conf manual page.
# (Type "man xorg.conf" at the shell prompt.)
#
# This file is automatically updated on xserver-xorg package upgrades *only*
# if it has not been modified since the last upgrade of the xserver-xorg
# package.
#
# Note that some configuration settings that could be done previously
# in this file, now are automatically configured by the server and settings
# here are ignored.
#
# If you have edited this file but would like it to be automatically updated
# again, run the following command:
#   sudo dpkg-reconfigure -phigh xserver-xorg

Section "Device"
   Identifier   "Configured Video Device"
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
   Identifier   "Configured Monitor"
EndSection

Section "Screen"
   Identifier   "Default Screen"
   Monitor      "Configured Monitor"
   Device      "Configured Video Device"
EndSection

THIS IS NOT ABOUT GETTING INTERNET TO WORK, BUT ABOUT THE SCREEN RESOLUTION.

### Post by 3rdalbum on 2009-07-02
In your Section "Screen", under the Monitor line, you need something like this:

```

    SubSection     "Display"
        Modes      "1920x1080"
    EndSubSection

```

Instead of "1920x1080", you need to put a resolution that you are comfortable with.

One common mistake made by new users running in virtualisation is to try and install a graphics card driver, causing Ubuntu to drop back to safe graphics mode. The virtualisation software emulates a generic video card; it does not "pass through" access to the real graphics card. This is done because graphics cards are not virtualisation-aware. So, DON'T install a graphics card driver, just type in a mode (probably 1024x768 would be a good next step).

---

### Post by Mastermind1980 on 2009-07-02
# xorg.conf (X.Org X Window System server configuration file)
#
# This file was generated by dexconf, the Debian X Configuration tool, using
# values from the debconf database.
#
# Edit this file with caution, and see the xorg.conf manual page.
# (Type "man xorg.conf" at the shell prompt.)
#
# This file is automatically updated on xserver-xorg package upgrades *only*
# if it has not been modified since the last upgrade of the xserver-xorg
# package.
#
# Note that some configuration settings that could be done previously
# in this file, now are automatically configured by the server and settings
# here are ignored.
#
# If you have edited this file but would like it to be automatically updated
# again, run the following command:
#   sudo dpkg-reconfigure -phigh xserver-xorg

Section "Device"
    Identifier    "Configured Video Device"
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
    Identifier    "Configured Monitor"
EndSection

Section "Screen"
    Identifier    "Default Screen"
    Monitor        "Configured Monitor"
    SubSection     "Display"
        Modes      "1024x768"
    EndSubSection
    Device        "Configured Video Device"
EndSection

### Post by Tonyr63 on 2009-07-03
Hi


I Think you need to enter a H_sync and V_sync value in the monitor section.  Do you know what kind of monitor you have and what is the graphics adapter type and capability?  It is not always easy to get this information which are needed to know how to configure the xorg.conf.

I've got a feeling that for a resolution of 1024x768 you need a entry like this in the Monitor section:

HorizSync	30-64
VertRefresh     60-90

If you google your monitor type & modeline you will arrive at threads that will give you the info you need to edit the xorg.conf file.

I had a similar issue with a Compaq Laptop Presario 1200 which I initially had no display and later just 800x600 on 70% of the screen space and I resolved with the correct modeline settings derived from a search of similar issues on web threads.

Best of luck and i think you will get it working.

;)

---

### Post by txcrackers on 2009-07-03
If you're running in VirtualBox, you need to install the VirtualBox "Guest" applications/drivers **first** before trying to change the resolution.

---
